question_text: A piece of ribbon folded five times is placed along a 30 cm long measuring scale as shown in Fig. 10.2. The length of the ribbon is between
answer_explanation: The folded piece measures roughly 25.5 cm (from 2.0 cm to 27.5 cm on the scale). Since it is folded 5 times, total length = 25.5 x 5 = 127.5 cm, which equals 1.275 m. This falls in the range 1.25 m to 1.35 m.

subject: Science
question_text: Paheli moves on a straight road from point A to point C. She takes 20 minutes to cover a certain distance AB and 30 minutes to cover the rest of distance BC. She then turns back and takes 30 minutes to cover the distance CB and 20 minutes to cover the rest of the distance to her starting point. She makes 5 rounds on the road the same way. Paheli concludes that her motion is
answer_key: rectilinear and periodic both.
answer_explanation: Her motion is rectilinear because she is moving along a straight road. It is periodic because she takes exactly the same total time (100 minutes) to complete each round trip 5 times.

subject: Science
question_text: Bholu and Golu are playing in a ground. They start running from the same point A in the ground and reach point B at the same time by following the paths marked 1 and 2 respectively as shown in Fig. 10.3. Which of the following is/are true for the given situation. As compared to Golu, Bholu covers a
options: longer distance but with a lower speed.
longer distance with a higher speed.
shorter distance with a lower speed.
shorter distance with a higher speed.
answer_key: longer distance with a higher speed.
answer_explanation: Assuming Bholu follows the curved path (1) and Golu follows the straight path (2). Path 1 is longer than Path 2. Since they reach at the same time, Bholu covered a longer distance in the same time, meaning he had a higher speed.

subject: Science
question_text:
Write one example for each of the following type of motion.
(i) Rectilinear
(ii) Circular
(iii) Periodic
(iv) Circular and periodic
answer_key:
(i) Rectilinear: A car moving on a straight road.
(ii) Circular: A point on the blade of a moving ceiling fan.
(iii) Periodic: The motion of a pendulum or a swing.
(iv) Circular and periodic: The motion of the hands of a clock.
answer_explanation: Examples of different types of motion.

subject: Science
question_text: The photograph given as Fig. 10.7 shows a section of a grille made up of straight and curved iron bars. How would you measure the length of the bars of this section, so that the payment could be made to the contractor?
answer_key:
1. For the straight iron bars, a measuring tape or a metre scale can be used directly.
2. For the curved iron bars, a thread can be placed along the curve to match its exact path. The length of the thread used can then be measured using a scale or tape.
answer_explanation: Measuring curved lengths.

subject: Science
question_text:
Four children measure the length of a table which was about 2 m. Each of them used different ways to measure it.
(i) Sam measured it with a half metre long thread.
(ii) Gurmeet measured it with a 15 cm scale from her geometry box.
(iii) Reena measured it using her hand span.
(iv) Salim measured it using a 5 m long measuring tape.
Which one of them would get the most accurate length? Give reason for your answer.
answer_key: Salim would get the most accurate length because he used a standard 5 m measuring tape. It is standard and long enough to measure the 2 m table in a single continuous stretch, eliminating additive errors. (Reena used non-standard body parts; Sam and Gurmeet had to measure multiple times adding potential for error).
answer_explanation: Understanding the importance of standard and appropriate tools.

subject: Science
question_text: While travelling in a train, it appears that the trees near the track are moving whereas co-passengers appear to be stationary. Explain the reason.
answer_key: This happens due to relative motion. The co-passengers and the observer are moving at the same speed and in the same direction with the train, meaning their positions relative to each other do not change. Conversely, stationary trees have a changing relative position to the fast-moving train, making them appear to move backward.
answer_explanation: Understanding frames of reference.

subject: Science
question_text: How are the motions of a wheel of a moving bicycle and a mark on the blade of a moving electric fan different? Explain.
answer_key:
1. The mark on the blade of a moving electric fan executes pure circular motion around a fixed central point; it does not change its overall location.
2. The wheel of a moving bicycle executes both circular or rotational motion (turning on its axle) AND rectilinear (translatory) motion (as it rolls forward in a straight line on the ground).
answer_explanation: Differentiating complex motions.
provenance_excerpt: How are the motions of a wheel of a moving bicycle and a mark on the blade of a moving electric fan different? Explain.

subject: Science
question_text: Three students measured the length of a corridor and reported their measurements. The values of their measurements were different. What could be the reason for difference in their measurements? (Mention any three)
answer_key:
1. They might have used non-standard units (e.g., footsteps or arm length), which vary from person to person.
2. They could have made parallax errors by not viewing the measuring tape straight on.
3. The measuring device might not have been placed perfectly straight along the corridor, or its zero mark could have been broken or misaligned.
answer_explanation: Evaluating sources of error in measurement.

subject: Science
question_text: Boojho was riding in his bicycle along a straight road. He classified the motion of various parts of the bicycle as (i) rectilinear motion, (ii) circular motion and (iii) both rectilinear as well as circular motion. Can you list one part of the bicycle for each type of motion? Support your answer with reason.
answer_key:
(i) Rectilinear motion: The bicycle frame or handle. It moves forward in a straight line without spinning.
(ii) Circular motion: The pedals. They move in circles around their axle relative to the rider.
(iii) Both rectilinear and circular motion: The wheels. They rotate on their axle (circular) while moving forward on the road (rectilinear).
answer_explanation: Application of motion concepts to a complex machine.
